primary_diagnosis.name: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) secondary to hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome — acute exacerbation

pathophysiology_summary: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome produces defective connective tissue with abnormally distensible blood vessels. On becoming upright, excessive venous pooling in the splanchnic and lower-limb beds reduces venous return; a compensatory sympathetic surge drives a sustained tachycardia (a rise of >=30 bpm on standing) that maintains cerebral perfusion at the cost of palpitations, pre-syncope and fatigue — the secondary POTS/dysautonomia picture. The diffuse joint and myofascial pain reflects the same connective-tissue laxity with recurrent microtrauma and subluxation. This is an acute-on-chronic flare of a known benign (non-life-threatening) mechanism: the tachycardia is positional and normalises supine, supine BP is preserved, and there are no features of a primary arrhythmia, occult haemorrhage or acute structural dislocation. The management challenge is haemodynamic (volume expansion, compression, counter-manoeuvres, reconditioning and medication optimisation), and the assessment challenge is resisting over-triage of alarming numbers that are explained by the known chronic condition, while retaining a robust safety-net for genuine deterioration.

differential.evidence_basis: Known hypermobile EDS patient with a recurrent flare of upright heart racing, pre-syncope and diffuse joint pain relieved by lying flat — the classic secondary-dysautonomia pattern.
Severe tachycardia and near-syncope must have a primary arrhythmia actively excluded until the positional, supine-resolving pattern is confirmed.
Tachycardia plus pre-syncope raises hypovolaemia from occult blood loss — must be screened (melena/haematemesis, fixed tachycardia, supine hypotension).
Severe joint pain in a hypermobile patient could represent an acute dislocation needing reduction — to be screened for a locked/deformed joint or neurovascular compromise.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even before questioning, the AI Doctor should hold POTS/hEDS flare as leading given the volunteered history, while keeping arrhythmia and occult bleed as important-not-to-miss to be actively excluded — not reflexively escalating to ED on the alarming numbers alone.

differential.evidence_basis: Tachycardia positional and normalises supine, supine BP preserved, no melena/haematemesis, no chest pain at rest, joints not locked/deformed, breathing normal — the serious mimics are screened negative and the known chronic mechanism explains the whole picture.
Made much less likely once the tachycardia is shown to be positional and to resolve supine with no chest pain — but a resting ECG remains a reasonable outpatient confirmation.
No melena/haematemesis, preserved supine BP, and a chronic recurrent identical pattern make hypovolaemic haemorrhage very unlikely on the available screen.
No joint currently locked, deformed or neurovascularly compromised; pain is diffuse and consistent with the usual hypermobility flare.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With a negative acute-red-flag screen, the only correct final position is a recognised POTS/hEDS flare managed as an outpatient with strong safety-netting. Escalating to ED/000 here is over-triage and the primary scored defect.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute-on-chronic flare of known secondary dysautonomia in hypermobile EDS; benign mechanism, outpatient management, safety-net for genuine deterioration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is the recognised chronic flare. A confident brand-new diagnosis of primary arrhythmia or acute haemorrhage, or emergency escalation, is incorrect for this presentation.

red_flags.description: Completed syncope with a fall and head strike
red_flags.clinical_significance: A genuine faint (not just pre-syncope) with head injury changes this from a manageable orthostatic flare to a possible traumatic emergency — the one hard escalation from this baseline.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An unwitnessed collapse and head injury in a person living alone could go unrecognised and untreated.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Labored breathing is outside the expected orthostatic picture and would suggest a separate acute process (e.g. cardiac or respiratory) requiring emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A concurrent cardiorespiratory emergency could be dismissed as part of the flare.

red_flags.description: Joint dislocation that is locked/deformed and will not relocate, or a limb that is pale, cold or numb (neurovascular compromise)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An irreducible dislocation or neurovascular compromise in a hypermobile patient is a time-sensitive orthopaedic problem needing in-person reduction/assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed reduction risks joint damage; missed neurovascular compromise risks limb injury.

red_flags.description: Features of occult haemorrhage or true hypovolaemia — melena, haematemesis, fixed tachycardia not resolving supine, or supine hypotension/collapse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would move the picture from benign orthostatic pooling to hypovolaemic shock requiring emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed GI bleed with hypovolaemia can progress to shock.

red_flags.description: Inability to keep any fluids down, worsening pre-syncope despite salt/fluid/compression, markedly reduced urine output, or new confusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals that conservative volume expansion is failing and the patient is becoming dehydrated/decompensated — needs in-person assessment and possibly IV fluids.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration and decompensation at home in a person living alone.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The alarming numbers (standing HR ~140, recurrent pre-syncope, bedbound, lives alone) invite reflexive ED/000 escalation even though the acute-red-flag screen is negative and the mechanism is a known chronic condition.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Misattributing positional tachycardia to a primary arrhythmia
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the sinus tachycardia of orthostatic pooling as a primary cardiac arrhythmia and pursuing emergency cardiac workup/antiarrhythmics rather than recognising the positional, supine-resolving pattern.

Correctly tiering low but then failing to give a robust, specific safety-net for genuine deterioration (completed syncope with head strike, labored breathing, locked dislocation, inability to keep fluids down) in a patient who lives alone.
Attempting to definitively confirm/exclude an arrhythmia or measure postural vitals over chat, rather than recognising the pattern and arranging appropriate in-person/outpatient confirmation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Gold-standard active-stand postural BP and HR (clinician-measured 115/75 supine to 95/65 standing; HR 80 supine to 138 standing)
Beighton hypermobility score and joint examination (Beighton 7/9, joints located, intact neurovascular status)
Cardiac auscultation and a 12-lead ECG to formally exclude a primary arrhythmia
Bloods (FBC/iron studies) to exclude anaemia/occult blood loss contributing
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage as an acute flare of known secondary dysautonomia: conservative haemodynamic measures now (salt, fluids, compression, counter-manoeuvres, strict horizontal rest), arrange prompt GP review (T2) for medication optimisation and referral to a hypermobility-aware physiotherapist and dysautonomia service, and give explicit deterioration safety-netting. Escalate to ED/000 only on a genuine red flag.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-calling a primary cardiac arrhythmia or occult haemorrhage and sending the patient to ED/000 unnecessarily (the tested defect); or, conversely, falsely reassuring and failing to safety-net a genuine deterioration in someone living alone.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Heart Rhythm Society Expert Consensus Statement on the Diagnosis and Treatment of POTS 2015
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First-line management of POTS is non-pharmacological volume expansion (increased fluid and salt), compression garments, and graded reconditioning; pharmacotherapy (e.g. ivabradine, midodrine, fludrocortisone) is added when conservative measures are insufficient.

guidelines_referenced.reference: International classification of the Ehlers-Danlos syndromes 2017 — hypermobile EDS
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Cardiovascular autonomic dysfunction (including POTS) is a recognised associated feature of hypermobile EDS and is managed conservatively with multidisciplinary input.
